1	---
2	id: REF-001
3	title: "Specification-Driven Development (SDD)"
4	kind: reference
5	source_url: https://github.com/github/spec-kit/blob/main/spec-driven.md
6	source_author: GitHub spec-kit
7	accessed: 2025-09-14
8	summary: "Argues that specifications, not code, should be the primary development artifact, with implementation generated from precise, executable specs."
9	tags: [spec-driven, sdd, methodology]
10	---
11	
12	# Specification-Driven Development (SDD)
13	
14	## The Power Inversion
15	
16	For decades, code has been king. Specifications served code—they were the scaffolding we built and then discarded once the "real work" of coding began. We wrote PRDs to guide development, created design docs to inform implementation, drew diagrams to visualize architecture. But these were always subordinate to the code itself. Code was truth. Everything else was, at best, good intentions. Code was the source of truth, as it moved forward, and spec's rarely kept pace. As the asset (code) and the implementation are one, it's not easy to have a parallel implementation without trying to build from the code.
17	
18	Spec-Driven Development (SDD) inverts this power structure. Specifications don't serve code—code serves specifications. The (Product Requirements Document-Specification) PRD isn't a guide for implementation; it's the source that generates implementation. Technical plans aren't documents that inform coding; they're precise definitions that produce code. This isn't an incremental improvement to how we build software. It's a fundamental rethinking of what drives development.
19	
20	The gap between specification and implementation has plagued software development since its inception. We've tried to bridge it with better documentation, more detailed requirements, stricter processes. These approaches fail because they accept the gap as inevitable. They try to narrow it but never eliminate it. SDD eliminates the gap by making specifications or and their concrete implementation plans born from the specification executable. When specifications to implementation plans generate code, there is no gap—only transformation.
21	
22	This transformation is now possible because AI can understand and implement complex specifications, and create detailed implementation plans. But raw AI generation without structure produces chaos. SDD provides that structure through specifications and subsequent implementation plans that are precise, complete, and unambiguous enough to generate working systems. The specification becomes the primary artifact. Code becomes its expression (as an implementation from the implementation plan) in a particular language and framework.
23	
24	In this new world, maintaining software means evolving specifications. The intent of the development team is expressed in natural language ("**intent-driven development**"), design assets, core principles and other guidelines . The **lingua franca** of development moves to a higher-level, and code is the last-mile approach.
25	
26	Debugging means fixing specifications and their implementation plans that generate incorrect code. Refactoring means restructuring for clarity. The entire development workflow reorganizes around specifications as the central source of truth, with implementation plans and code as the continuously regenerated output. Updating apps with new features or creating a new parallel implementation because we are creative beings, means revisiting the specification and creating new implementation plans. This process is therefore a 0 -> 1, (1', ..), 2, 3, N.
27	
28	The development team focuses in on their creativity, experimentation, their critical thinking.
29	
30	## The SDD Workflow in Practice

input.content:
---
id: REF-005
title: "GitHub Spec Kit"
kind: reference
source_url: https://github.com/github/spec-kit
source_organization: GitHub
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Open-source toolkit from GitHub demonstrating spec-driven development workflows where executable specifications drive AI-assisted code generation."
tags: [spec-driven, sdd, tooling, github]
---

# GitHub Spec Kit

GitHub's reference implementation of Specification-Driven Development (SDD). Provides templates, prompts, and workflow scaffolding for treating PRDs and technical plans as the primary, executable artifacts that generate implementation. Cited as a foundational tool/methodology source for DDx and HELIX framing.

---
id: REF-006
title: "AWS Kiro"
kind: reference
source_url: https://kiro.dev
source_organization: Amazon Web Services
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "AWS's spec-driven AI development environment that turns natural-language requirements into structured specs, design, and implementation steps before coding."
tags: [spec-driven, ide, aws, ai-development]
---

# AWS Kiro

Kiro is an AWS spec-first AI IDE that materializes a "specs → design → tasks → code" pipeline. Used as evidence that major platform vendors are converging on spec-driven, document-anchored workflows for AI agents — a key piece of the software-factory framing.

---
id: REF-007
title: "Software Factories: Assembling Applications with Patterns, Models, Frameworks, and Tools"
kind: reference
source_url: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/software-factories-assembling-applications-with-patterns-models-frameworks-and-tools/
source_author: Jack Greenfield, Keith Short, Steve Cook, Stuart Kent
source_organization: Microsoft / Wiley
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "2004 book proposing 'software factories' — a model-driven, pattern-based approach to industrializing software construction via reusable assets, schemas, and tooling."
tags: [software-factory, model-driven, methodology, foundational]
---

# Software Factories (Greenfield & Short, 2004)

Foundational text framing software construction as an industrialized assembly process driven by domain-specific languages, models, and reusable assets. Provides the historical lineage for DDx's "software factory" framing: documents, templates, and tooling as the production line.

---
id: REF-008
title: "Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts"
kind: reference
source_url: https://aclanthology.org/2024.tacl-1.9/
source_author: Nelson F. Liu, Kevin Lin, John Hewitt, Ashwin Paranjape, Michele Bevilacqua, Fabio Petroni, Percy Liang
source_organization: TACL 2024
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Empirical study showing LLM performance degrades sharply when relevant information sits in the middle of long contexts, with U-shaped recall curves favoring start and end positions."
tags: [context, long-context, evaluation, research]
---

# Lost in the Middle (Liu et al., TACL 2024)

Establishes that long-context models do not use their full context uniformly: information placed in the middle is recalled far less reliably than information at the start or end. Cited to justify DDx/HELIX's emphasis on small, governing artifacts and curated context windows over dumping everything into the prompt.

---
id: REF-009
title: "Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Degrades LLM Performance"
kind: reference
source_url: https://research.trychroma.com/context-rot
source_organization: Chroma Research
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "2025 study quantifying performance degradation as prompt length grows, even within advertised context windows; effect persists across frontier models."
tags: [context, long-context, evaluation, research]
---

# Context Rot (Chroma, 2025)

Empirical evaluation showing that effective context utilization decays well before models hit their nominal context limit. Reinforces the case for context discipline (governing artifacts, focused beads, retrieval over stuffing) in document-driven agent workflows.

---
id: REF-010
title: "Human and Computer Control of Undersea Teleoperators"
kind: reference
source_url: https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/citations/ADA057655
source_author: Thomas B. Sheridan, William L. Verplank
source_organization: MIT Man-Machine Systems Laboratory
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "1978 technical report introducing the canonical 10-level scale of human–computer automation, from full manual to full autonomy, foundational to human-in-the-loop design."
tags: [automation, human-in-the-loop, foundational, hci]
---

# Sheridan & Verplank — Levels of Automation (1978)

Origin of the 10-level automation taxonomy used across HCI and autonomous systems research. Provides the conceptual frame for HELIX's supervisory dispatch and DDx's bounded-action model: how much agency the agent gets, and where the human stays in the loop.

---
id: REF-011
title: "OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications (2025)"
kind: reference
source_url: https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/
source_organization: OWASP
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Industry-standard taxonomy of the top security risks for LLM applications, including prompt injection, insecure output handling, training data poisoning, and excessive agency."
tags: [security, llm, owasp, threat-model]
---

# OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025)

Authoritative threat-model reference for LLM-integrated applications. Used to anchor DDx/HELIX's security posture: bounded actions, sandboxed execution, output validation, and agency limits all map to specific OWASP LLM categories.

---
id: REF-012
title: "EchoLeak — CVE-2025-32711"
kind: reference
source_url: https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2025-32711
source_organization: NIST NVD / Aim Security
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Zero-click prompt-injection vulnerability in Microsoft 365 Copilot enabling silent data exfiltration via crafted email content rendered in agent context."
tags: [security, prompt-injection, cve, copilot]
---

# EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711)

Real-world demonstration of indirect prompt injection escalating to zero-click data exfiltration in a production AI assistant. Cited as concrete evidence that LLM agents need bounded actions, output filtering, and trust boundaries — not just careful prompting.

---
id: REF-013
title: "MAST: A Multi-Agent System Failure Taxonomy"
kind: reference
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.13657
source_author: Mert Cemri et al.
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "2025 taxonomy of failure modes in LLM-based multi-agent systems, classifying coordination, role-confusion, verification, and termination failures across real benchmarks."
tags: [multi-agent, failure-modes, evaluation, research]
---

# MAST — Multi-Agent System Failure Taxonomy (2025)

Empirical study cataloguing how multi-agent LLM systems fail in practice — most failures are coordination/verification breakdowns, not raw model capability gaps. Justifies DDx's emphasis on explicit handoffs (beads), clear acceptance criteria, and review gates over loosely-coordinated agent swarms.

---
id: REF-014
title: "Data on the Outside vs. Data on the Inside"
kind: reference
source_url: https://queue.acm.org/detail.cfm?id=3415014
source_author: Pat Helland
source_organization: ACM Queue
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Distinguishes immutable, versioned data flowing between services ('outside') from mutable internal state ('inside'), arguing the outside is the contract that survives change."
tags: [data, contracts, distributed-systems, foundational]
---

# Helland — Data on the Outside vs. Data on the Inside

Foundational essay framing inter-service data as immutable, versioned facts that act as contracts. Cited to motivate DDx's treatment of governing artifacts (specs, beads, ADRs) as the durable "outside" data that agents and humans both consume.

---
id: REF-015
title: "FrugalGPT: How to Use Large Language Models While Reducing Cost and Improving Performance"
kind: reference
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.05176
source_author: Lingjiao Chen, Matei Zaharia, James Zou
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Introduces cost-tier cascades, prompt adaptation, and LLM approximation to deliver GPT-4-class quality at a fraction of the cost via tiered routing."
tags: [routing, cost, multi-model, research]
---

# FrugalGPT (Chen, Zaharia, Zou, 2023)

Seminal paper on cost-tiered LLM routing: cheap models attempt first, escalate to stronger models only when needed. Foundational reference for DDx's cost-tiered work pattern (cheap models do, strong models review) and Fizeau-style routing.

---
id: REF-016
title: "Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback"
kind: reference
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17651
source_author: Aman Madaan et al.
source_organization: NeurIPS 2023
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Demonstrates that LLMs can improve their own outputs by generating, critiquing, and revising — a single-model loop that lifts quality across diverse tasks."
tags: [agents, self-critique, research, evaluation]
---

# Self-Refine (Madaan et al., NeurIPS 2023)

Establishes iterative self-critique-and-revise as a robust quality-lifting pattern for LLMs. Underwrites HELIX's review gates and DDx's review-step escalation: generate, critique, revise — preferably with a stronger reviewer than producer.

---
id: REF-017
title: "Literate Programming"
kind: reference
source_url: https://academic.oup.com/comjnl/article/27/2/97/343244
source_author: Donald E. Knuth
source_organization: The Computer Journal (1984)
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Knuth's foundational essay arguing that programs should be written primarily for humans to read, with code and explanatory prose interleaved as a single artifact."
tags: [methodology, documentation, foundational]
---

# Knuth — Literate Programming (1984)

Foundational argument for treating programs as documents written for humans, with executable code as a derivative artifact. Direct intellectual ancestor of document-driven development: the document is the program; the binary is the build output.

---
id: REF-018
title: "EvalPlus: Rigorous Evaluation of LLM-Synthesized Code"
kind: reference
source_url: https://github.com/evalplus/evalplus
source_author: Jiawei Liu, Chunqiu Steven Xia, Yuyao Wang, Lingming Zhang
source_organization: NeurIPS 2023
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Augmented HumanEval/MBPP benchmarks with substantially expanded test suites, exposing that pass@1 numbers on the original benchmarks overstate model correctness."
tags: [evaluation, benchmarks, code-generation, research]
---

# EvalPlus

Demonstrates that mainstream code-generation benchmarks have under-tested oracles and that pass rates collapse under stricter test suites. Cited to argue that DDx's acceptance criteria must be deterministic, executable, and exhaustive — not "pass the happy path" tests.

---
id: REF-019
title: "Behavior-Driven Development and the Cucumber Lineage"
kind: reference
source_url: https://cucumber.io/docs/bdd/
source_author: Dan North (BDD origin); Aslak Hellesøy (Cucumber)
source_organization: Cucumber.io
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "BDD reframes tests as executable specifications written in a domain-readable Given-When-Then form; Cucumber operationalized it as a widely adopted tool family."
tags: [bdd, testing, executable-specs, methodology]
---

# BDD / Cucumber Lineage

Behavior-Driven Development established that human-readable, scenario-style specifications can double as executable tests. Direct precursor to DDx's acceptance-criteria-as-contract pattern: each AC item is the deterministic shape a scenario takes in the bead model.

---
id: REF-020
title: "Designing Data-Intensive Applications"
kind: reference
source_url: https://dataintensive.net/
source_author: Martin Kleppmann
source_organization: O'Reilly Media (2017)
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Comprehensive synthesis of distributed-systems principles: replication, partitioning, consistency, stream processing, and the trade-offs that govern scalable data systems."
tags: [distributed-systems, foundational, data]
---

# Kleppmann — Designing Data-Intensive Applications

Standard reference for distributed-systems trade-offs informing DDx's storage, sync, and contract decisions (bead JSONL interchange, git-subtree sync, event-sourced bead history).

---
id: REF-021
title: "Event Sourcing"
kind: reference
source_url: https://martinfowler.com/eaaDev/EventSourcing.html
source_author: Martin Fowler
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Architectural pattern that records all state changes as an immutable, ordered event log; current state is a derived projection rebuildable at any time."
tags: [architecture, event-sourcing, foundational]
---

# Fowler — Event Sourcing

Reference for treating state as a replayable log of immutable events. Backs DDx's bead-update model and execution-evidence trail: the log is the truth; everything else is a projection.

---
id: REF-022
title: "Critical Path Method (CPM) and PERT"
kind: reference
source_url: https://www.pmi.org/learning/library/critical-path-method-pert-7588
source_author: Morgan R. Walker, James E. Kelley (CPM); U.S. Navy Special Projects Office (PERT)
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "1957–1958 project-scheduling techniques modeling work as a directed acyclic graph of tasks with dependencies, durations, and a longest (critical) path."
tags: [scheduling, dag, project-management, foundational]
---

# CPM / PERT (1957–1958)

Origin of DAG-based project scheduling. Direct lineage for DDx's bead dependency graph: tasks as nodes, dependencies as edges, "ready" beads as the frontier whose predecessors have closed.

---
id: REF-023
title: "Bazel — A Fast, Scalable, Multi-Language Build System"
kind: reference
source_url: https://bazel.build/about
source_organization: Google / Bazel project
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Build system that models work as a hermetic action graph (DAG) with content-addressed inputs, enabling reproducible, incremental, and remote-cacheable builds."
tags: [build-systems, dag, tooling]
---

# Bazel

Industry-grade DAG-based build system. Reference for treating work as a hermetic, content-addressed graph — the same shape DDx applies to bead execution: typed inputs, typed outputs, deterministic re-execution.

---
id: REF-024
title: "Dask — Parallel Computing with Task Graphs"
kind: reference
source_url: https://docs.dask.org/en/stable/graphs.html
source_organization: Dask project
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Python parallel-computing library that represents computations as task graphs (DAGs) and schedules them across cores or clusters with dynamic, dependency-aware execution."
tags: [task-graphs, dag, parallel, tooling]
---

# Dask Task Graphs

Reference implementation of dynamic DAG scheduling for heterogeneous workloads. Cited as evidence that the task-graph + ready-frontier model generalizes well beyond builds — it's the same primitive driving DDx's bead queue.

---
id: REF-025
title: "Language Server Protocol (LSP)"
kind: reference
source_url: https://microsoft.github.io/language-server-protocol/
source_organization: Microsoft
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Open protocol that decouples editors from language tooling: m editors × n languages collapses to m + n integrations via a shared JSON-RPC contract."
tags: [protocols, integration, foundational, tooling]
---

# Language Server Protocol

Canonical example of collapsing an m×n integration matrix to m+n via a stable contract. Cited to motivate DDx's contract-first surfaces (bead JSONL, agent harness contract, MCP) — keep the protocol stable, swap implementations freely.

---
id: REF-026
title: "The Protection of Information in Computer Systems"
kind: reference
source_url: https://www.cs.virginia.edu/~evans/cs551/saltzer/
source_author: Jerome H. Saltzer, Michael D. Schroeder
source_organization: Proceedings of the IEEE (1975)
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Foundational paper enumerating security design principles, including least privilege, fail-safe defaults, complete mediation, and economy of mechanism."
tags: [security, foundational, principles]
---

# Saltzer & Schroeder (1975)

Origin of the canonical security design principles, especially least privilege. Direct backing for DDx/HELIX bounded actions: agents get the smallest set of capabilities required to satisfy a bead, not arbitrary shell access.

---
id: REF-027
title: "Building Effective Agents"
kind: reference
source_url: https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents
source_organization: Anthropic
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Anthropic's 2024 guidance distinguishing workflows (predictable, composed) from agents (autonomous, looping) and recommending the simplest pattern that works."
tags: [agents, design-patterns, anthropic]
---

# Anthropic — Building Effective Agents

Practitioner guidance arguing that most agent problems are best solved by orchestrated workflows, not free-running autonomous agents. Underwrites HELIX's bounded supervisory dispatch and DDx's preference for explicit bead handoffs over open-ended loops.

---
id: REF-028
title: "Empirical Study of Documentation Drift in Software Projects"
kind: reference
source_url: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-023-10301-2
source_organization: Empirical Software Engineering, Springer (2023)
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Quantifies how technical documentation diverges from implementation over time, the rate of drift, and which documentation types decay fastest."
tags: [documentation, drift, empirical, research]
---

# Documentation Drift (Springer, 2023)

Empirical evidence that documentation rots quickly when it is decoupled from the build/test loop. Motivates DDx's stance that governing artifacts must be executable or referenced from runtime checks (acceptance criteria, contracts, evidence trails) — not prose to be read once and forgotten.

---
id: REF-029
title: "Multi-Model Routing — 2026 Industry Consensus"
kind: reference
source_url: https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/model-routing
source_organization: OpenRouter / industry survey
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Synthesis of 2026 vendor and OSS practice converging on cost/quality-tiered model routing with fallback chains, live capability discovery, and per-task model choice."
tags: [routing, multi-model, industry, cost]
---

# Multi-Model Routing — 2026 Consensus

By 2026, multi-model routing (cheap-first, escalate-on-failure, capability-aware) is standard across OpenRouter, AWS Bedrock, Vercel AI SDK, and OSS gateways. Cited as the industry context for DDx's endpoint-first routing redesign and Fizeau service.

---
id: REF-030
title: "Portability Lineage: OCI, POSIX, SQL"
kind: reference
source_url: https://opencontainers.org/
source_organization: Open Container Initiative; IEEE/Open Group (POSIX); ISO/IEC (SQL)
accessed: 2026-05-01
summary: "Three landmark standards — POSIX (1988), SQL (ISO/IEC 9075, 1987), and OCI (2015) — that delivered cross-vendor portability by stabilizing a contract surface, not implementations."
tags: [standards, portability, contracts, foundational]
---

# OCI / POSIX / SQL — Portability Lineage

Each of these standards proved that a stable, narrow contract surface beats a feature-complete vendor lock-in. Cited as the historical evidence base for DDx's contract-first stance: the value lives in the protocol (bead JSONL, agent harness contract), and implementations are interchangeable.
